FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Baseline N = 11,878 (White = 52.0%, Hispanic = 20.3%, Black = 15.0%, Asian = 2.1%, Other = 10.5%; Female = 47.8%), tracked across Years 1–4

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age of acquisition (parent-reported, multiple waves); meets B1 qualifying criterion for smartphone variable with separate coefficient

FT_IC3.evidence: Multi-level modeling with hierarchical linear regression; time × informant interaction testing discrepancy change over time; direct effect of informant (parent vs. child) on internalizing problems modeled

FT_IC4.evidence: Main effect regression coefficients reported: b = -0.21, SE = .042, p < .001, 95% CI [-.29, -.23] for full sample trajectory; multi-group model coefficients across smartphone age groups (Table 3)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal multi-level design across 4 waves; examination of parent-child informant discrepancy trajectories as outcome

FT_IC6.evidence: No detected preprint, abstract, or duplicate publication

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting internalizing problems (parent-child discrepancy thereof), not the dependent variable. Paper has SM → non-SM-outcome analysis (SM → informant discrepancy in internalizing problems), which directly disqualifies FT-EC7 per § 3.1(a).
notes: Paper examines smartphone ownership age as predictor of parent-child discrepancy in reporting youth internalizing problems across early adolescence. Multi-group models stratify by age-of-acquisition categories (8 & under, 9–10, 11–12, 13–14, No Phone).

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, T1 N = 11,787 with longitudinal follow-up across 3 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Electronic media use (overall and type-specific social electronic media); meets B1 criterion for separately reported coefficients by media type

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal regression models examining bidirectional associations; separate analyses by electronic media type and gender; cross-lagged design

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients and significance tests reported for associations between media use at T1 and family conflict at T2 and later timepoints

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; 3-year longitudinal design examining prospective associations between electronic media use and family conflict

FT_EC7_reason: Electronic media use is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting family conflict (outcome), not the dependent variable. Paper has SM/media → non-SM-outcome analysis (SM → family conflict), which disqualifies EC7 per § 3.1(a). Additionally, family conflict is not in the closed demographic-only predictor set, and analysis includes bidirectional cross-lagged effects (B20).
notes: Bidirectional longitudinal study of electronic media use and family conflict. Findings indicate that overall electronic media use at T1 predicted increased family conflict at later timepoints; reverse association not supported. Gender-stratified analyses show social media use effects differ between girls and boys.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Baseline N ≈ 12,000 (M age = 9.9; White = 52.0%, Hispanic = 20.3%, Black = 15.0%, Asian = 2.1%, Other = 10.5%; Female = 47.8%), tracked across 4 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use as primary exposure; time-varying predictor of cognitive performance trajectories; meets B1 criterion for separate coefficient

FT_IC3.evidence: Parallel process latent growth curve modeling; trajectory coupling examined via covariances between social media growth and cognitive ability growth factors (crystallized abilities, inhibitory control/attention, processing speed)

FT_IC4.evidence: Latent growth curve coefficients including trajectory coupling estimates; model parameter estimates for growth factors and their correlations reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal parallel-process modeling of coupled trajectories across 4 years of early adolescence

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use is the INDEPENDENT variable in analyses predicting cognitive performance trajectories, not the dependent variable. Paper has SM → non-SM-outcome analysis (SM growth → cognitive ability growth coupling), disqualifying EC7 per § 3.1(a). EC7 requires SM as sole dependent variable across all analyses; this paper uses SM as IV alongside cognitive outcomes as DVs.
notes: Longitudinal parallel-process latent growth curve study. Results indicate negative coupling between social media use trajectory and crystallized abilities and inhibitory control/attention trajectories (trade-off pattern); positive coupling with processing speed (practice transfer). All three papers from batch 10 meet inclusion criteria (FT-IC1 through FT-IC6); none qualify as EC7-only exclusions.
